examples.explanation: Start with candidate = 4. Check divisibility: 4%1=0 ✓, 4%2=0 ✓, 4%3=1 ✗ (not divisible). Increment candidate to 5: 5%1=0 ✓, 5%2=1 ✗. Candidate 6: 6%1=0 ✓, 6%2=0 ✓, 6%3=0 ✓, 6%4=2 ✗. Candidate 7: 7%1=0 ✓, 7%2=1 ✗. Candidate 8: 8%1=0 ✓, 8%2=0 ✓, 8%3=2 ✗. Candidate 9: 9%1=0 ✓, 9%2=1 ✗. Candidate 10: 10%1=0 ✓, 10%2=0 ✓, 10%3=1 ✗. Candidate 11: 11%1=0 ✓, 11%2=1 ✗. Candidate 12: 12%1=0 ✓, 12%2=0 ✓, 12%3=0 ✓, 12%4=0 ✓. All divisible, so LCM = 12.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n * max_val)
approaches.summary: Iterate through numbers starting from n, checking divisibility by all integers from 1 to n using a loop. Stop when a number is found that is divisible by all, as it computes the LCM directly by testing candidates.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * max_val), where max_val is the LCM result, which grows rapidly

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force LCM(1..n) Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete solution using brute force iteration to find the smallest number divisible by all integers from 1 to n.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding LCM(1..n) works by systematically testing candidate numbers starting from n and checking if each is divisible by all integers from 1 to n. The intuition is straightforward: since the LCM must be at least n (as it must be divisible by n), we start searching from n upward. For each candidate number, we verify divisibility by checking if candidate % i == 0 for all i from 1 to n. The first candidate that passes all divisibility checks is the LCM. This works because the LCM is defined as the smallest positive integer divisible by all numbers in the range. The algorithm grows inefficient as n increases because the LCM grows rapidly (approximately exponentially), leading to many iterations. Time complexity is O(n * max_val) where max_val is the LCM result, and space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach connects directly to the problem statement by implementing the exact definition of LCM through exhaustive testing. Common mistakes include starting the search from 1 instead of n (inefficient), not handling edge cases like n=1, and using incorrect divisibility checks. The real code pattern involves a while loop for candidate generation and an inner for loop for divisibility verification.

examples.trace: Start with candidate = 4. Check divisibility: 4%1=0 ✓, 4%2=0 ✓, 4%3=1 ✗ (not divisible). Increment candidate to 5: 5%1=0 ✓, 5%2=1 ✗. Candidate 6: 6%1=0 ✓, 6%2=0 ✓, 6%3=0 ✓, 6%4=2 ✗. Candidate 7: 7%1=0 ✓, 7%2=1 ✗. Candidate 8: 8%1=0 ✓, 8%2=0 ✓, 8%3=2 ✗. Candidate 9: 9%1=0 ✓, 9%2=1 ✗. Candidate 10: 10%1=0 ✓, 10%2=0 ✓, 10%3=1 ✗. Candidate 11: 11%1=0 ✓, 11%2=1 ✗. Candidate 12: 12%1=0 ✓, 12%2=0 ✓, 12%3=0 ✓, 12%4=0 ✓. All divisible, so LCM = 12.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Divisibility Check for All Numbers 1 to n
ladders.desc: Helper function that checks if a given number is divisible by all integers from 1 to n.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core verification step in the brute force LCM approach. The intuition is that to determine if a candidate number is the LCM, we need to confirm it's divisible by every integer in the range [1, n]. This is done by iterating through i from 1 to n and checking if candidate % i == 0. If any i fails this check, the candidate is not divisible by all numbers, and we return false. Only if all checks pass do we return true. The algorithm is simple: initialize a loop from 1 to n, check divisibility for each i, and return false immediately upon failure to optimize performance. Time complexity is O(n) per candidate check, and space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by being called repeatedly for each candidate number until one passes. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases (e.g., n=0, though n≥1 in this problem), using incorrect loop bounds, or not optimizing with early exit. The real code pattern involves a for loop with a conditional return.

examples.trace: Check i=1: 12%1=0 ✓. i=2: 12%2=0 ✓. i=3: 12%3=0 ✓. i=4: 12%4=0 ✓. All checks pass, return true.
Check i=1: 10%1=0 ✓. i=2: 10%2=0 ✓. i=3: 10%3=1 ✗ (not divisible). Return false immediately without checking i=4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Candidate Testing from n Upward
ladders.desc: The central technique of generating candidate numbers and testing them until the LCM is found.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the brute force search strategy. The intuition is that the LCM of 1..n must be at least n, so we start searching from n and incrementally test larger numbers. For each candidate, we use the divisibility check (from L1) to see if it's divisible by all numbers 1 to n. If yes, we've found the LCM and return it. If not, we increment the candidate and continue. This works because we're exhaustively testing all possible numbers in ascending order, guaranteeing we find the smallest one that satisfies the condition. The algorithm: initialize candidate = n, while true, check divisibility for candidate, if true return candidate, else candidate++. Time complexity depends on how many candidates are tested; in worst case, it's O(n * max_val). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by forming the main loop of the full solution. Common mistakes include starting from 1 (inefficient), not incrementing correctly, or infinite loops if no candidate is found (though mathematically one always exists). The real code pattern involves a while loop with a break condition.

examples.trace: Start candidate = 3. Check divisibility: 3%1=0 ✓, 3%2=1 ✗, so false. Increment to 4: 4%1=0 ✓, 4%2=0 ✓, 4%3=1 ✗. Increment to 5: 5%1=0 ✓, 5%2=1 ✗. Increment to 6: 6%1=0 ✓, 6%2=0 ✓, 6%3=0 ✓, return 6.
Start candidate = 2. Check divisibility: 2%1=0 ✓, 2%2=0 ✓, return 2 immediately.

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking Divisibility of a Number by Another
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of determining if one integer is divisible by another using the modulo operator.
ladders.explanation: This building block is fundamental to verifying divisibility in the LCM problem. The intuition is that an integer a is divisible by b if the remainder when a is divided by b is zero. Mathematically, a % b == 0. This works because division yields no remainder only when b exactly divides a. The algorithm is straightforward: compute a % b using the modulo operator and compare to 0. Time complexity is O(1) for fixed-size integers, and space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 and L1 by being the basic operation within the divisibility check loop. Common mistakes include confusing modulo with integer division, not handling edge cases like b=0 (though in this problem, b ranges from 1 to n, so b≥1), or using floating-point operations which can introduce precision errors. The real code pattern is a simple conditional check.

examples.trace: Compute 10 % 2 = 0. Since remainder is 0, 10 is divisible by 2. Return true.
Compute 7 % 3 = 1. Since remainder is not 0, 7 is not divisible by 3. Return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Operation for Remainder
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of computing the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation, denoted as a % b, computes the remainder when a is divided by b. The intuition is that in integer division, a can be expressed as a = b * q + r, where q is the quotient and r is the remainder with 0 ≤ r < b. The modulo operation returns r. This works because it captures the part of a that is not evenly divisible by b. For example, 10 % 3 = 1 because 10 = 3*3 + 1. The algorithm is typically built into programming languages; manually, it can be computed as a - b * (a // b) where // is integer division. Time and space complexity are O(1) for fixed-size integers. This feeds into L3 as the core computation for divisibility checks. Common mistakes include misunderstanding negative operands (in this problem, inputs are positive), confusing with division, or not knowing language-specific behavior. The real code pattern is using the % operator directly.

examples.trace: Divide 10 by 3: quotient = 3, remainder = 10 - 3*3 = 1. So 10 % 3 = 1.
Divide 8 by 2: quotient = 4, remainder = 8 - 2*4 = 0. So 8 % 2 = 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Multiples
ladders.desc: Prerequisite concept of how integers divide evenly into multiples.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the basic idea that an integer a is a multiple of b if a can be divided by b with no remainder. The intuition is that multiples are products of an integer with another integer; for example, multiples of 3 are 3, 6, 9, etc. When a is a multiple of b, we say b divides a, or a is divisible by b. This works because division is the inverse of multiplication: if a = b * k for some integer k, then a is a multiple of b. The algorithm to check this is to see if a divided by b yields an integer result, which in programming is done via modulo as in L4. Time and space complexity are trivial for understanding. This feeds into L4 and above by providing the mathematical basis for divisibility. Common mistakes include confusing multiples with factors, or not understanding that 0 is a multiple of every integer (since 0 = b * 0). The real code pattern is conceptual, but leads to using modulo checks.

examples.trace: Since 12 = 4 * 3, 12 is a multiple of 4. Check: 12 divided by 4 equals 3, an integer, so true.
10 divided by 3 is approximately 3.33, not an integer. Since 10 is not equal to 3 times any integer, it is not a multiple of 3. So false.

approaches.name: Prime Factorization O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Compute the LCM by finding the maximum exponent for each prime number up to n, using prime factorization for each integer. Multiply the primes raised to their max exponents to get the LCM, leveraging the mathematical property that LCM can be derived from prime powers.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to prime factorization for each number up to n
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing prime factors or a sieve

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute LCM(1..n) via Prime Factorization
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find smallest number divisible by all integers from 1 to n using prime factorization approach.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that the LCM of numbers 1 through n can be expressed as the product of prime powers, where for each prime p ≤ n, we take the highest power of p that appears in the factorization of any number from 1 to n. This works because LCM must contain all prime factors from all numbers with their maximum exponents to ensure divisibility. The algorithm first generates all primes up to n using the Sieve of Eratosthenes in O(n log log n) time. Then for each prime p, it finds the maximum exponent e such that p^e ≤ n by repeatedly multiplying p while the result ≤ n. The LCM is computed by multiplying all p^e modulo a large number to prevent overflow. The time complexity is O(n log n) dominated by prime factorization considerations, though actual implementation is O(n log log n + n log n) for sieve and exponent calculation. Space is O(n) for the sieve array. This connects to the mathematical foundation that any integer can be uniquely represented as product of primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). Common mistakes include not handling large results with modulo, incorrect exponent calculation, or inefficient prime generation. The pseudocode: 1. Generate primes up to n using sieve. 2. Initialize result = 1. 3. For each prime p: a. exponent = 0, power = p b. While power ≤ n: exponent += 1; power *= p c. result = (result * p^exponent) % MOD 4. Return result.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Maximum Prime Exponent for LCM
ladders.desc: Determine the highest power of each prime p that contributes to LCM(1..n).
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine finds for a given prime p and upper bound n, the maximum integer exponent e such that p^e ≤ n. The intuition is that in the range 1 to n, the largest number that has p^e as a factor is p^e itself (or multiples), so p^e must be ≤ n to appear in the range. For LCM, we need the highest power of p that appears in any number's factorization within 1..n. The algorithm: initialize exponent = 0, power = p. While power ≤ n: increment exponent, multiply power by p. Return exponent. Time: O(log_p n) which is O(log n) worst-case when p=2. Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the exponent for each prime to compute the final product. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in the loop condition, integer overflow in power multiplication. Pseudocode: function maxExponent(p, n): exponent = 0; power = p; while power ≤ n: exponent += 1; power *= p; return exponent.

examples.trace: Initialize exponent=0, power=2. Check: 2 ≤ 10 → exponent=1, power=4. Check: 4 ≤ 10 → exponent=2, power=8. Check: 8 ≤ 10 → exponent=3, power=16. Check: 16 > 10 → stop. Return exponent=3.
Initialize exponent=0, power=5. Check: 5 ≤ 30 → exponent=1, power=25. Check: 25 ≤ 30 → exponent=2, power=125. Check: 125 > 30 → stop. Return exponent=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Prime Factorization for LCM Construction
ladders.desc: Use prime factorization to build LCM from individual numbers' factorizations.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that LCM can be computed by taking the union of prime factors from all numbers with maximum exponents. For numbers 1 to n, instead of factorizing each number separately (which would be O(n log n)), we can directly determine the maximum exponent for each prime p by finding the largest p^e ≤ n. This leverages the mathematical property that for any number k in 1..n, its prime factorization uses primes ≤ k with exponents such that the prime power ≤ k. The maximum exponent across all numbers for prime p is precisely the largest e with p^e ≤ n. The algorithm: generate primes up to n; for each prime p, compute max exponent e; accumulate p^e into result. Time: O(n log log n) for sieve + O(π(n) log n) for exponent calc, where π(n) is prime-counting function. Space: O(n). This is the central technique in L0. Common mistakes: confusing with GCD or using inefficient factorization. Pseudocode: result = 1; for prime in primesUpTo(n): e = maxExponent(prime, n); result *= prime^e; return result.

examples.trace: Primes ≤ 8: [2,3,5,7]. For p=2: max exponent is 3 (2^3=8 ≤ 8, 2^4=16 > 8). For p=3: max exponent is 2 (3^2=9 > 8, so 3^1=3 ≤ 8 → exponent=1). For p=5: max exponent is 1 (5^1=5 ≤ 8). For p=7: max exponent is 1 (7^1=7 ≤ 8). LCM = 2^3 * 3^1 * 5^1 * 7^1 = 8*3*5*7=840.

ladders.title: Building Block — Generate All Primes Up To n Using Sieve
ladders.desc: Implement Sieve of Eratosthenes to efficiently list primes ≤ n.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed because the LCM approach requires iterating through all primes up to n. The Sieve of Eratosthenes works by initially assuming all numbers from 2 to n are prime, then iteratively marking multiples of each prime as composite. The intuition: if a number is not marked composite by any smaller prime, it must be prime. Algorithm: create boolean array isPrime[0..n] initialized to True for indices ≥2. For p from 2 to sqrt(n): if isPrime[p] is True: for multiple from p*p to n step p: mark isPrime[multiple]=False. Collect all p where isPrime[p] is True. Time: O(n log log n) due to harmonic series of primes. Space: O(n). This feeds into L2 by providing the prime list. Common mistakes: not optimizing by starting from p*p, incorrect array bounds, including 0 and 1. Pseudocode: function sieve(n): isPrime = [True]*(n+1); isPrime[0]=isPrime[1]=False; for p in range(2, int(sqrt(n))+1): if isPrime[p]: for multiple in range(p*p, n+1, p): isPrime[multiple]=False; return [i for i in range(2, n+1) if isPrime[i]].

examples.trace: Initialize isPrime[0..10] all True except 0,1. p=2: mark multiples 4,6,8,10 as False. p=3: mark 9 as False (6 already False). p=4: not prime, skip. p=5: > sqrt(10)≈3.16, stop. Primes are indices with True: 2,3,5,7.

Initialize isPrime[0..20]. p=2: mark 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 False. p=3: mark 9,15 False (6,12,18 already False). p=5: mark 25>20, none. p=7: > sqrt(20)≈4.47, stop. Collect primes: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Prime Power (Exponentiation)
ladders.desc: Calculate p^e efficiently for integer base and exponent.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation computes the power of a prime, needed for multiplying into the LCM. The intuition is repeated multiplication: p^e = p * p * ... * p (e times). For efficiency, use iterative multiplication rather than built-in exponentiation to control overflow with modulo if needed. Algorithm: initialize result = 1; for i in range(e): result *= p; return result. Time: O(e) which is O(log n) in our context. Space: O(1). This feeds into L3 and above for computing p^maxExponent. Common mistakes: using exponentiation that causes overflow, incorrect loop bounds. Pseudocode: function power(p, e): result = 1; for i in range(e): result *= p; return result.

examples.trace: Initialize result=1. i=0: result=1*2=2. i=1: result=2*2=4. i=2: result=4*2=8. Return 8.
Initialize result=1. i=0: result=1*5=5. i=1: result=5*5=25. Return 25.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand prime numbers and basic divisibility rules.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples: 2,3,5,7,11. Divisibility: a number a divides b (written a|b) if b/a is an integer. The intuition for LCM: the least common multiple of numbers must be divisible by each number, meaning it contains all prime factors from each number. For instance, 12 is divisible by 4 and 6 because 12=2^2*3^1, 4=2^2, 6=2*3. This foundational concept underpins the entire approach: we use primes as building blocks. No complex algorithm here—just recognition. This feeds into L4 by providing the base numbers for exponentiation. Common mistakes: confusing prime with composite, misunderstanding divisibility. Pseudocode: not applicable—conceptual understanding.

examples.trace: Check each number 2-10: 2 (divisors 1,2) → prime. 3 (1,3) → prime. 4 (1,2,4) → not prime. 5 (1,5) → prime. 6 (1,2,3,6) → not prime. 7 (1,7) → prime. 8 (1,2,4,8) → not prime. 9 (1,3,9) → not prime. 10 (1,2,5,10) → not prime.
12 ÷ 4 = 3, which is an integer, so 4 divides 12.

approaches.name: Sieve and Exponentiation O(n log log n)
approaches.summary: Use the Sieve of Eratosthenes to find all primes up to n, then for each prime, compute the highest power less than or equal to n. Multiply these prime powers to get the LCM, optimizing by avoiding repeated factorization through precomputed primes.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log log n) for the sieve plus O(n) for exponent calculations

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute LCM(1,2,...,n) using Sieve and Exponentiation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find the smallest number divisible by all integers from 1 to n using prime factorization via sieve.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that the LCM of numbers 1 through n equals the product of the highest powers of all primes ≤ n. For example, for n=10, primes are 2,3,5,7. The highest power of 2 ≤ 10 is 2³=8 (since 2⁴=16>10), for 3 it's 3²=9, and for 5 and 7 it's the primes themselves. LCM(1..10)=8×9×5×7=2520. This avoids computing pairwise LCMs, which would be inefficient. The Sieve of Eratosthenes (O(n log log n)) identifies all primes up to n efficiently. Then, for each prime p, we find the maximum exponent e such that pᵉ ≤ n by repeatedly multiplying p while ≤ n. The product of all pᵉ gives the LCM. Time complexity is O(n log log n) for the sieve plus O(n) for exponent calculations, dominated by the sieve. Space is O(n) for the boolean array. Connection to L1: This uses the prime exponent calculation subroutine from L1. Common mistakes include integer overflow for large n (use modulo if needed, though not specified here), incorrect sieve implementation (marking non-primes), or missing that the highest power is pᵉ ≤ n, not pᵉ < n. Real code pattern: def lcm_upto(n): sieve = [True]*(n+1); sieve[0]=sieve[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if sieve[i]: for j in range(i*i, n+1, i): sieve[j]=False; result=1; for p in range(2, n+1): if sieve[p]: power=p; while power*p <= n: power*=p; result*=power; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sieve primes up to 5. Initialize sieve [True]*6. Mark 0,1 as False. Start from 2: mark multiples 4 as False. 3: no multiples ≤5. 4 already False. 5: no multiples ≤5. Primes: 2,3,5. Step 2: For each prime, find highest power ≤5. For 2: powers 2,4 (2²=4≤5, 2³=8>5), highest=4. For 3: powers 3 (3²=9>5), highest=3. For 5: powers 5 (5²=25>5), highest=5. Step 3: Multiply: 4×3×5=60. Output: 60.

Step 1: Sieve primes up to 10. Initialize sieve [True]*11. Mark 0,1 False. From 2: mark 4,6,8,10 False. 3: mark 9 False. 4 already False. 5: no multiples ≤10. 6 False, 7: no multiples ≤10. 8 False, 9 False, 10 False. Primes: 2,3,5,7. Step 2: Highest powers ≤10. For 2: 2,4,8 (2³=8≤10, 2⁴=16>10), highest=8. For 3: 3,9 (3²=9≤10, 3³=27>10), highest=9. For 5: 5, highest=5. For 7: 7, highest=7. Step 3: Multiply: 8×9×5×7=2520. Output: 2520.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Highest Prime Power ≤ n
ladders.desc: For a given prime p and limit n, find the largest pᵉ such that pᵉ ≤ n.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is crucial because the LCM(1..n) is the product of these highest prime powers. The intuition is that to cover all multiples of p up to n, we need the maximum exponent e where pᵉ does not exceed n. For example, for p=2, n=10, powers are 2,4,8,16... but 16>10, so highest is 8 (2³). This ensures that in the LCM, the factor p appears sufficiently to divide all numbers 1..n that contain p. The algorithm: initialize power=p; while power*p ≤ n: power=power*p; return power. This loops O(log n) times per prime. Time complexity per prime is O(log n), but overall across primes it's O(n) since total primes ~ n/log n. Space is O(1). Connection to L0: This is called for each prime from the sieve to compute the contribution to the LCM. Common mistakes: using exponentiation via pow(p,e) which might overflow, or incorrect loop condition (e.g., power ≤ n instead of power*p ≤ n). Real code pattern: def highest_power(p, n): power=p; while power*p <= n: power*=p; return power.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize power=3. Check: power*p=3*3=9 ≤20? Yes, so update power=9. Step 2: Check: power*p=9*3=27 ≤20? No, stop. Output: 9 (3²).
Step 1: Initialize power=5. Check: power*p=5*5=25 ≤30? Yes, update power=25. Step 2: Check: power*p=25*5=125 ≤30? No, stop. Output: 25 (5²).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Prime Factorization for LCM via Highest Powers
ladders.desc: Understand that LCM(1..n) = product of highest prime powers ≤ n for all primes ≤ n.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that the least common multiple of numbers 1 through n can be derived from prime factorization without computing all numbers' factorizations individually. For any prime p, the highest power pᵉ that appears in any number from 1 to n is the maximum e such that pᵉ ≤ n. This is because if pᵉ ≤ n, then there exists a number (pᵉ itself) that contributes this power, and no number ≤ n has a higher power of p. Thus, to ensure the LCM is divisible by all numbers, it must include pᵉ. Multiplying over all primes gives the LCM. For example, n=6: primes 2,3,5. Highest powers: for 2, 2²=4≤6 (since 2³=8>6); for 3, 3¹=3≤6 (3²=9>6); for 5, 5¹=5≤6. Product: 4×3×5=60, which is LCM(1..6). This reduces the problem to finding primes and their highest powers. Time to compute this conceptually is O(π(n) log n) where π(n) is prime count, but efficient via sieve. Space is O(1) for the product. Connection to L1: This logic uses the highest power computation. Common mistakes: confusing with LCM of individual numbers (e.g., computing pairwise LCMs), or missing that primes > n/2 but ≤ n (like 7 for n=10) still contribute as themselves. Real code pattern: Conceptual—no direct code, but understanding guides implementation.

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify primes ≤4: 2,3. Step 2: Highest power of 2 ≤4: 2²=4 (since 2³=8>4). Step 3: Highest power of 3 ≤4: 3¹=3 (since 3²=9>4). Step 4: Multiply: 4×3=12. Output: 12, which is LCM(1,2,3,4).
Step 1: Primes ≤8: 2,3,5,7. Step 2: Highest powers: 2³=8≤8 (2⁴=16>8), 3²=9>8 so 3¹=3, 5¹=5, 7¹=7. Step 3: Multiply: 8×3×5×7=840. Output: 840.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sieve of Eratosthenes to Find Primes up to n
ladders.desc: Implement the Sieve of Eratosthenes to efficiently list all prime numbers ≤ n.
ladders.explanation: The Sieve of Eratosthenes is an ancient algorithm to find all primes up to a limit n. The intuition is to iteratively mark multiples of primes as non-prime, starting from 2. Initially, assume all numbers from 2 to n are prime. For each number i from 2 to √n, if i is still marked prime, then mark all multiples of i (i², i²+i, ...) as non-prime. This works because any composite number ≤ n has a prime factor ≤ √n. After processing, the unmarked numbers are primes. For example, for n=10: start with [2..10] prime. i=2: mark 4,6,8,10 non-prime. i=3: mark 9 non-prime. i=4 already non-prime. i=5 > √10≈3.16, stop. Primes: 2,3,5,7. Time complexity is O(n log log n) due to harmonic series of primes. Space is O(n) for a boolean array. Connection to L2: This provides the primes needed for the LCM factorization. Common mistakes: not optimizing by starting from i² (since smaller multiples are already marked), or incorrect loop bounds (up to √n). Real code pattern: def sieve(n): is_prime=[True]*(n+1); is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if is_prime[i]: for j in range(i*i, n+1, i): is_prime[j]=False; return [i for i in range(2, n+1) if is_prime[i]].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize is_prime[0..15] all True except 0,1 False. Step 2: i=2 (≤√15≈3.87): mark multiples from 4 to 15 step 2: 4,6,8,10,12,14 False. Step 3: i=3: mark multiples from 9 to 15 step 3: 9,12,15 False (12 already False). Step 4: i=4 > √15, stop. Step 5: Collect primes: indices with True: 2,3,5,7,11,13. Output: [2,3,5,7,11,13].

Step 1: Initialize is_prime[0..20] True except 0,1 False. Step 2: i=2 (≤√20≈4.47): mark 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 False. Step 3: i=3: mark 9,12,15,18 False (12,18 already False). Step 4: i=4 already False, skip. i=5 > √20, stop. Step 5: Primes: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19. Output: [2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterative Multiplication to Compute Powers
ladders.desc: Multiply a number by itself repeatedly to compute powers without using exponentiation functions.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves computing pᵉ by starting with p and multiplying by p repeatedly e-1 times. For example, to compute 2³, start with 2, multiply by 2 to get 4, multiply by 2 to get 8. The intuition is that exponentiation can be done iteratively: result=1; for i in range(e): result*=p. However, in our context, we compute until exceeding a limit n, so we use a while loop: power=p; while power*p ≤ n: power*=p. This is efficient and avoids floating-point issues. Time complexity is O(e) per power, which is O(log n) since e ≤ log_p n. Space is O(1). Connection to L3: This is used in the sieve subroutine to find highest powers. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., multiplying one extra time), or using pow(p,e) which might be less intuitive for limit checks. Real code pattern: def compute_power(p, e): result=1; for _ in range(e): result*=p; return result. Or for limit: power=p; while power*p <= limit: power*=p.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result=1. Step 2: Multiply by 3: result=3. Step 3: Multiply by 3: result=9. Step 4: Multiply by 3: result=27. Step 5: Multiply by 3: result=81. Output: 81.

Step 1: Start with power=5. Check: 5*5=25 ≤100? Yes, update power=25. Step 2: Check: 25*5=125 ≤100? No, stop. Output: 125 (but note 125>100, so in context we'd use previous power 25; here showing multiplication steps).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers are and the basics of divisibility.
ladders.explanation: Prime numbers are natural numbers greater than 1 that have no positive divisors other than 1 and themselves. For example, 2,3,5,7 are primes; 4 is not because it is divisible by 2. Divisibility means that a number a divides b if b/a is an integer, denoted a|b. The fundamental theorem of arithmetic states that every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime powers (e.g., 12=2²×3¹). This foundation is crucial because the LCM problem relies on prime factorization: the least common multiple of numbers is the product of the highest powers of all primes that appear in their factorizations. For instance, LCM of 4 (2²) and 6 (2¹×3¹) is 2²×3¹=12. Understanding primes helps in algorithms like the sieve. Time to check primality naively is O(√n) per number. Space is O(1). Connection to L4: This underpins why we care about primes in multiplication for powers. Common mistakes: considering 1 as prime (it's not), or misunderstanding unique factorization. Real code pattern: def is_prime(num): if num<2: return False; for i in range(2, int(num**0.5)+1): if num%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: 7>1, so proceed. Step 2: Check divisors from 2 to √7≈2.65, i.e., 2. Step 3: 7%2=1 ≠0. Step 4: No other divisors, so 7 is prime. Output: True.
Step 1: 9>1, proceed. Step 2: Check divisors from 2 to √9=3, i.e., 2,3. Step 3: 9%2=1 ≠0. Step 4: 9%3=0, so divisible by 3. Step 5: Thus, 9 is not prime. Output: False.

approaches.name: Recursive GCD Formula O(n log min(a,b))
approaches.summary: Compute LCM iteratively using the formula LCM(a,b) = a * b / GCD(a,b), starting from 1 and building up to n by repeatedly applying the formula. This leverages the associative property of LCM to combine numbers step-by-step.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log min(a,b)) due to repeated GCD computations using Euclidean algorithm

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute LCM(1,2,...,n) Using Iterative GCD Formula
ladders.desc: Complete solution using LCM(a,b) = a*b/GCD(a,b) iteratively from 1 to n
ladders.explanation: This problem requires computing the least common multiple (LCM) of all integers from 1 to n. The key insight is that LCM is associative: LCM(1,2,...,n) = LCM(LCM(1,2,...,n-1), n). We can compute this iteratively using the formula LCM(a,b) = a * b / GCD(a,b). Starting with lcm = 1, we iterate i from 2 to n, updating lcm = lcm * i / GCD(lcm, i). This works because LCM is commutative and associative, allowing us to combine numbers in any order. The Euclidean algorithm computes GCD efficiently in O(log min(a,b)) time, giving overall O(n log min(a,b)) time complexity. Space is O(1) as we only store the current lcm value. Common mistakes include integer overflow (use long integers or Python's arbitrary precision), forgetting to divide by GCD before multiplication (which causes overflow), and incorrect handling of edge cases like n=0 or n=1. The algorithm connects to L1 by using the GCD function as a key subroutine.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Euclidean Algorithm for GCD
ladders.desc: Compute greatest common divisor using Euclidean algorithm
ladders.explanation: The Euclidean algorithm computes the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers a and b. The intuition is based on the property that GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). If we repeatedly replace the larger number with the remainder when divided by the smaller number, we eventually reach a remainder of 0, at which point the other number is the GCD. This works because any common divisor of a and b also divides a mod b. The algorithm runs in O(log min(a,b)) time since the numbers reduce quickly (at least by half every two iterations in worst case). Space is O(1) for iterative implementation. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loops when b=0 (base case), and integer overflow in languages with fixed precision. This connects to L0 as GCD is needed for the LCM formula, and to L2 as it's the core operation in computing LCM.

examples.trace:
Step1: a=48, b=18, 48 mod 18 = 12
Step2: a=18, b=12, 18 mod 12 = 6
Step3: a=12, b=6, 12 mod 6 = 0
Step4: b=0, so GCD=6

Step1: a=17, b=13, 17 mod 13 = 4
Step2: a=13, b=4, 13 mod 4 = 1
Step3: a=4, b=1, 4 mod 1 = 0
Step4: b=0, so GCD=1

ladders.title: Core Logic — LCM Computation Using GCD Formula
ladders.desc: Compute LCM of two numbers using LCM(a,b) = a*b/GCD(a,b)
ladders.explanation: The least common multiple (LCM) of two integers a and b is the smallest positive integer divisible by both a and b. The formula LCM(a,b) = a * b / GCD(a,b) works because any common multiple must contain all prime factors of both numbers. The product a*b contains all factors, but GCD(a,b) contains the overlapping factors that would otherwise be counted twice. Dividing by GCD removes this duplication, giving the minimal common multiple. For example, with a=6 (2×3) and b=8 (2³), GCD=2, product=48, LCM=48/2=24 which is 2³×3. Time complexity is O(log min(a,b)) dominated by GCD computation. Space is O(1). Common mistakes include integer overflow (compute as a/GCD(a,b)*b to minimize overflow), division by zero (handle when a or b is 0), and incorrect order of operations. This connects to L1 as it uses GCD, and to L0 as it's the fundamental operation in the iterative solution.

examples.trace:
Step1: Compute GCD(12,18)=6
Step2: Compute product: 12*18=216
Step3: Divide by GCD: 216/6=36
Result: LCM=36

Step1: Compute GCD(15,20)=5
Step2: Compute product: 15*20=300
Step3: Divide by GCD: 300/5=60
Result: LCM=60

ladders.title: Building Block — Associative Property of LCM
ladders.desc: Understanding that LCM(a,b,c) = LCM(LCM(a,b),c)
ladders.explanation: The associative property of LCM states that when computing LCM of three or more numbers, we can combine them in any grouping. Formally, LCM(a,b,c) = LCM(LCM(a,b),c) = LCM(a,LCM(b,c)). This works because LCM finds the minimal number containing all prime factors. Grouping doesn't change the final set of required prime factors. For example, LCM(2,3,4): factors needed are 2² and 3. LCM(2,3)=6 (2×3), then LCM(6,4)=12 (2²×3). Direct computation: prime factors of 2,3,4 are 2² and 3, giving 12. This property enables iterative computation. Time complexity for computing LCM of k numbers iteratively is O(k log min(values)). Space is O(1). Common mistakes include assuming commutativity without associativity (it has both), incorrect grouping order (order doesn't matter), and not understanding why this enables incremental computation. This connects to L2 as it explains how to extend two-number LCM to multiple numbers, and to L0 as it justifies the iterative approach.

examples.trace:
Method1: LCM(LCM(2,3),4)
Step1: LCM(2,3)=6
Step2: LCM(6,4)=12
Method2: LCM(2,LCM(3,4))
Step1: LCM(3,4)=12
Step2: LCM(2,12)=12
Both give 12

Method1: LCM(LCM(3,4),5)
Step1: LCM(3,4)=12
Step2: LCM(12,5)=60
Method2: LCM(3,LCM(4,5))
Step1: LCM(4,5)=20
Step2: LCM(3,20)=60
Both give 60

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Multiplication
ladders.desc: Performing basic arithmetic operations on integers
ladders.explanation: Integer division and multiplication are fundamental arithmetic operations. Multiplication (a*b) computes the product of two integers. Integer division (a/b) computes the quotient when a is divided by b, discarding any remainder. In programming, these operations have O(1) time complexity for fixed-size integers, but can cause overflow when results exceed data type limits. For example, with 32-bit integers, maximum value is 2³¹-1 ≈ 2.1 billion. The key intuition for LCM computation is that we need to multiply numbers then divide by their GCD. To avoid overflow, we can rearrange as a/GCD(a,b)*b, doing division first when possible. Common mistakes include forgetting integer division truncates (use // in Python), overflow in languages like C++/Java (use long or BigInteger), and incorrect operator precedence. This connects to L3 as these operations are needed to compute LCM from GCD, and to L2 as they're part of the LCM formula.

examples.trace:
Multiplication: 12*3=36
Division: 12/3=4 (integer division)
Note: 12 divided by 3 equals 4 exactly

Multiplication: 7*2=14
Division: 7/2=3 (integer division, remainder 1 discarded)
7 ÷ 2 = 3 remainder 1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Multiples and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Basic concepts of multiples, common multiples, and divisibility
ladders.explanation: A multiple of an integer a is any integer that can be expressed as a*k where k is an integer. For example, multiples of 3 are 3, 6, 9, 12, etc. A common multiple of two numbers a and b is a number that is a multiple of both a and b. For example, common multiples of 2 and 3 are 6, 12, 18, etc. The least common multiple (LCM) is the smallest positive common multiple. Divisibility means one number can be divided by another without remainder: a divides b if b/a is an integer. These concepts are foundational because LCM finds the smallest number divisible by all given numbers. Intuition: if a number is divisible by both a and b, it must contain all prime factors from both numbers. Time/space considerations don't apply here as it's conceptual. Common mistakes include confusing multiples with factors, not recognizing that LCM is always at least as large as the largest number, and forgetting that every number is a multiple of 1. This connects to L4 as it explains why we need multiplication/division operations for LCM computation.

examples.trace:
Multiples of 2: 2,4,6,8,10,12,...
Multiples of 4: 4,8,12,16,20,...
Common multiples: 4,8,12,16,...
LCM is 4 (smallest common multiple)

examples.input: Check divisibility: Is 15 divisible by 3? Is 15 divisible by 4?
examples.trace:
15 ÷ 3 = 5 exactly, so 15 is divisible by 3
15 ÷ 4 = 3.75, not an integer, so 15 is not divisible by 4

testCases.input: Common multiples of 3 and 5 up to 30
LCM of 1 and any number n
Common multiples of 2 and 6 up to 20
Smallest number divisible by both 2 and 3
